class_schedule.topic: What Is Literature? Definitions and Debates
learning_objectives: Distinguish between literary and non-literary texts using formal criteria
Identify the major genres of literature: poetry, prose fiction, drama, and creative nonfiction
Understand the concept of the literary canon and its historical construction
readings: Eagleton, Literary Theory: An Introduction, Ch. 1 - 'What Is Literature?'
Selections from the Norton Anthology of English Literature, General Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: This course provides the foundational skills and frameworks you will use throughout your study of English literature. We begin with the most fundamental question: what makes a text 'literary'?
key_concepts: Literariness: defamiliarization and the foregrounding of language
Genre distinctions: poetry, prose fiction, drama, creative nonfiction
The literary canon: who decides what counts and why
Close reading as the primary skill of literary studies
The difference between reading for information and reading for interpretation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading exercise: comparing a newspaper article and a poem about the same event to identify what makes the poem 'literary'
setup_requirements: Norton Anthology of English Literature (any recent edition)
Composition notebook for in-class writing exercises

review.value: Viktor Shklovsky's concept that literature makes the familiar strange, forcing the reader to perceive the world anew rather than recognizing it automatically
The body of works traditionally considered the most important in a literary tradition, shaped by historical power structures and subject to ongoing revision

learning_objectives: Apply close reading techniques to a short poem
Identify and analyze literary devices including metaphor, imagery, and tone
Formulate an interpretive claim supported by textual evidence
readings: Dickinson, 'Because I could not stop for Death'
Barnet & Cain, A Short Guide to Writing About Literature, Ch. 2 - 'Writing About Literature'

lecture_notes.introduction: Close reading is the bread and butter of literary studies. Today we practice the discipline of attending carefully to what a text actually says and how it says it before leaping to interpretation.
key_concepts: Close reading methodology: observation before interpretation
Identifying literary devices: metaphor, simile, imagery, personification, irony
Tone and diction: how word choice creates meaning
The difference between summary and analysis
Formulating an interpretive thesis from textual evidence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Guided close reading of Dickinson's 'Because I could not stop for Death,' moving from line-by-line observation to an interpretive argument about the poem's treatment of mortality
setup_requirements: Printed copy of the Dickinson poem
Highlighters or colored pens for annotation

review.value: Effective close reading begins with noticing formal features — word choice, syntax, imagery, sound — before making claims about meaning
Every interpretive claim must be grounded in specific language from the text; unsupported assertions are opinions, not analysis

class_schedule.topic: Narrative Structure and Point of View
learning_objectives: Identify the elements of narrative structure: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution
Distinguish among first-person, second-person, third-person limited, and omniscient narration
Analyze how point of view shapes the reader's access to information and sympathy

lecture_notes.introduction: Every story is told from somewhere. Understanding who is telling the story, what they know, and what they choose to reveal is essential to understanding how fiction creates meaning.
key_concepts: Freytag's pyramid: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, denouement
Point of view types: first person, second person, third limited, third omniscient
Reliable vs. unreliable narration
Free indirect discourse: blending narrator and character perspective
How narrative structure creates suspense, irony, and surprise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of Chopin's 'The Story of an Hour' focusing on how the third-person limited perspective controls the reader's knowledge and creates dramatic irony
setup_requirements: Printed copy of 'The Story of an Hour'

review.value: A narrator whose credibility is compromised, requiring the reader to read against the narrator's account to understand the story's true meaning
A narrative technique where the narrator's voice merges with a character's thoughts without explicit attribution, common in Austen and Flaubert

learning_objectives: Scan a line of poetry to identify its metrical pattern
Distinguish among common poetic forms: sonnet, villanelle, free verse, blank verse
Analyze how sound devices — rhyme, alliteration, assonance — contribute to a poem's meaning
readings: Shakespeare, Sonnet 18 ('Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?')
Fussell, Poetic Meter and Poetic Form, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Poetry is the genre where language is most intensely compressed. Understanding how poets use form, meter, and sound is essential to reading poetry with precision rather than vague impressionism.
key_concepts: Metrical feet: iamb, trochee, anapest, dactyl, spondee
Scansion: marking stressed and unstressed syllables to reveal meter
Common forms: Shakespearean sonnet (three quatrains + couplet), Petrarchan sonnet (octave + sestet), villanelle, free verse
Sound devices: rhyme scheme, alliteration, assonance, consonance, enjambment
The relationship between form and content: why a poet chooses a particular form
lecture_notes.demonstration: Scanning Shakespeare's Sonnet 18 to identify iambic pentameter, analyzing where the meter breaks and why those disruptions carry meaning

review.value: A line of five iambic feet (unstressed-stressed), the dominant meter of English verse from Shakespeare through the Romantics
The continuation of a syntactic unit across a line break, creating tension between the visual line and the grammatical sentence

learning_objectives: Identify the conventions of dramatic structure: acts, scenes, stage directions, soliloquy
Analyze dialogue as a vehicle for characterization and conflict
Understand the tension between a play as a written text and as a performed event

lecture_notes.introduction: Drama is literature designed for performance. When we read a play, we must imagine it on stage — but we also have the advantage of studying the language with a closeness that performance does not always permit.
key_concepts: Aristotle's elements of tragedy: plot, character, thought, diction, spectacle, song
Dramatic irony: when the audience knows what the characters do not
The function of the chorus in Greek tragedy
Stage directions as authorial commentary and interpretive constraint
Soliloquy and aside: direct address to the audience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Antigone's confrontation with Creon, analyzing how the dialogue reveals both characters' values and the play's central thematic conflict between divine law and human authority
setup_requirements: Complete text of Antigone in translation
Handout on Aristotle's elements of tragedy

review.value: A situation in which the audience possesses information that a character does not, creating tension, humor, or pathos depending on context
Often translated as 'tragic flaw,' more accurately an error in judgment that leads to the protagonist's downfall in Aristotelian tragedy

class_schedule.topic: Research Methods and Writing the Literary Essay
learning_objectives: Navigate MLA citation format for literary scholarship
Construct a thesis-driven literary argument with integrated textual evidence
Evaluate secondary sources for credibility and relevance
readings: Sample student literary analysis essay (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Writing a literary essay is not summarizing what happens in a text. It is making an argument about how a text creates meaning — and supporting that argument with evidence from the text and from scholarly conversation.
key_concepts: Thesis construction: arguable, specific, and grounded in textual evidence
The ICE method: Introduce, Cite, Explain for integrating quotations
MLA format: in-text citations, Works Cited, formatting conventions
Primary vs. secondary sources in literary scholarship
Avoiding common pitfalls: plot summary, unsubstantiated claims, biographical fallacy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: students revise a weak thesis statement into a strong one, then practice integrating a quotation using the ICE method
setup_requirements: MLA Handbook or access to Purdue OWL MLA guide

review.value: Introduce the quotation with context, Cite the quotation with proper MLA formatting, Explain how the quotation supports your argument
A strong literary thesis makes a claim that a reasonable reader could disagree with; if no one would argue against it, it is an observation, not an argument

questions.question: Define 'defamiliarization' and explain how it applies to the Dickinson poem we studied in class. Provide a specific example from the text.
questions.answer: Defamiliarization is the literary technique of presenting familiar things in unfamiliar ways to force fresh perception. In 'Because I could not stop for Death,' Dickinson defamiliarizes death by personifying it as a courteous gentleman caller who takes the speaker on a leisurely carriage ride, transforming the terrifying into the civil and domestic.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of how the example demonstrates the concept

questions.question: What is the difference between a first-person narrator and a third-person limited narrator? How does the choice of point of view affect the reader's experience in 'The Story of an Hour'?
questions.answer: A first-person narrator is a character within the story who tells it using 'I,' while a third-person limited narrator stands outside the story but restricts knowledge to one character's perspective. In 'The Story of an Hour,' the third-person limited point of view allows the reader access to Mrs. Mallard's private thoughts of freedom while maintaining enough distance to create dramatic irony when other characters misinterpret her emotional state.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between first-person and third-person limited
Accurate application to 'The Story of an Hour'
Identifies dramatic irony as a consequence of the POV choice

questions.question: Scan the following line from Shakespeare's Sonnet 18 and identify its meter: 'Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?' Mark each syllable as stressed or unstressed.
questions.answer: shall I / com PARE / thee TO / a SUM / mer's DAY — iambic pentameter. The first foot may be read as a trochee (inversion), which is a common variation in the opening foot of an iambic line.

rubric.key: Notes the possible initial trochaic inversion

questions.question: Define dramatic irony and provide an example from Antigone, explaining how it intensifies the audience's experience.
questions.answer: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something a character does not. In Antigone, the audience knows from the beginning that Antigone has buried her brother, while Creon does not discover this until later. This creates tension during Creon's proclamations about punishing the unknown transgressor, because the audience watches him unknowingly condemn his own future daughter-in-law.

rubric.key: Specific and accurate example from Antigone
Explains how the irony affects the audience's experience

major_assignments.title: Close Reading Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 1,200-1,500 word close reading essay that makes an interpretive argument about one of the texts studied in weeks 1-3. The essay must use textual evidence to support a thesis about how the text creates meaning through its formal and stylistic features.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of close reading methodology
Construct a clear, arguable thesis grounded in textual evidence
Integrate quotations using the ICE method with proper MLA citation
Analyze formal features rather than merely summarizing content
deliverables: Works Cited page with primary text entry
Annotated passage submitted as appendix showing close reading process

criteria: Thesis is clear, specific, arguable, and stated early in the essay
Argument develops logically from paragraph to paragraph
Each body paragraph advances the thesis with a distinct claim
Conclusion extends or complicates the argument rather than merely restating it
Analysis focuses on formal features: diction, imagery, structure, sound, point of view
Quotations are well-chosen and directly support the argument
Analysis explains how textual details create meaning rather than merely identifying devices
Close reading demonstrates careful attention to the text's language
Prose is clear, precise, and free of grammatical errors
MLA format is followed correctly for in-text citations and Works Cited
Quotations are integrated smoothly using the ICE method
Essay meets the word count requirement without padding
Strong, arguable thesis demonstrating genuine interpretive insight
Sustained close reading grounded in specific textual evidence
Professional writing quality with correct MLA citation
Analysis of how formal features create meaning, not plot summary

major_assignments.title: Genre Comparison Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 1,500-2,000 word essay comparing how two texts from different genres (poetry, fiction, or drama) treat a shared theme. The essay must analyze how each genre's formal conventions shape the treatment of the theme differently.
objectives: Apply close reading skills across multiple genres
Analyze how genre conventions shape thematic expression
Construct a comparative argument using a point-by-point or block structure
Incorporate at least one secondary scholarly source
deliverables: 1,500-2,000 word comparative essay in MLA format
Works Cited page including primary texts and at least one secondary source
Outline submitted as appendix showing comparative structure

criteria: Comparison illuminates both texts rather than treating them in isolation
Genre-specific conventions are identified and analyzed, not just mentioned
Analysis explains how different genres create different effects with similar themes
Neither text is subordinated to the other; both receive substantive analysis
Secondary source is credible scholarly material, not a summary website
Source is integrated to support or complicate the argument, not merely cited
Student's own analysis remains the primary content of the essay
MLA citation is accurate for all sources
Essay structure supports the comparative argument effectively
Transitions between texts and between paragraphs are smooth and logical
Prose is clear, precise, and free of errors
Essay meets length requirements without filler
Insightful comparative argument revealing how genre shapes meaning
Strong close reading of both texts with balanced analysis
Effective integration of scholarly source material
Well-structured, clearly written essay meeting all formal requirements

participation_and_quizzes.description: In-class participation, close reading exercises, and quizzes assessing literary terminology and analytical skills

close_reading_essay.description: Individual close reading essay demonstrating foundational analytical skills

genre_comparison_essay.description: Comparative essay analyzing how genre conventions shape thematic expression

reading_responses.description: Weekly one-page reading responses demonstrating engagement with assigned texts

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering literary terminology, close reading, and genre analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. This is a discussion-based course; more than 3 unexcused absences will result in a full letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late essays are penalized 5% per day. Reading responses are not accepted late. Extensions require advance arrangement at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: All essays must be original individual work. Discussion of texts and ideas with classmates is encouraged, but written work must be your own. Cite any sources that influence your argument.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism — including uncited paraphrase and use of AI-generated text submitted as your own writing — will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to course texts (Norton Anthology or equivalent), word processing software for MLA formatting, and LMS access for submission and course materials.
